title: Chapter 4: The Archive and the Commission
content: I wake with mascara crusted to my lashes like iron filings and a throat that feels lined with sandpaper. Suki's couch. Still in the catering uniform — the black polyester shirt stiff now with dried sweat, the apron bunched under my spine like a reproach. Daylight cuts through the gap in the curtains with the particular cruelty of Saturday morning in June.
For three full seconds, I don't remember. Just the ceiling, the water stain shaped like a lung, the smell of Suki's nicotine-free vapor lingering in the fabric.
Then it comes back. All of it. Not in pieces — in a flood.

The coat on the auction block. The catalogue description, wrong, so wrong. My voice cutting through the ballroom like a blade I didn't know I was holding. Rowan Ashford turning, finding me, his eyes the color of things I've spent centuries trying to name. The hum spiking so hard I thought my teeth would crack.
And before that — the flash.

Firelight. A great hall. Timber beams black with age and smoke. A ring on a hand that might have been mine, emerald set in gold, catching the fire's glow. The smell of woodsmoke and wool and something else, something underneath, like stone after rain.
I haven't had a vision in nine years. Nine years since the last one, and that one was mild — a storefront in Harlem, 1971, already faded. This was different. This had *weight*.
I press my palms against my eyes until I see constellations.
Not now. Not now.

"Hey." Suki's voice, muffled. I hear her feet on the hardwood before I see her — she rounds the hallway corner in an oversized Columbia hoodie and boxer shorts, a mug in each hand. She sets one on the coffee table beside me and folds herself into the armchair across from the couch, tucking her legs beneath her. "You sounded like you were dying last night. I mean, more than usual when you come back from those gigs."
"Thanks for the couch."
"Thanks for not dying on it." She blows on her coffee. "Talked a lot in your sleep."
I go very still. "What did I say?"

"Something about a fire. And a name — Rowan? I figured it was some guy." She chews her gum, watching me with the particular attention she usually reserves for broken zippers and fraying seams. "You okay?"
I pick up the coffee. It's bitter and too hot, and I drink it anyway. "I'm always okay, Suki."

"Yeah, you always say that too." She doesn't push. Suki's gift is knowing exactly how far to go and then stopping, which is rarer than people think. She points at me with her mug. "There's toothpaste in the bathroom. And a clean shirt — the gray one you left here last month."
"Nothing's clean on me anymore."
"That's not funny."
"It wasn't a joke."
She makes a face — half sympathy, half exasperation — and I take the out she's offered. Bathroom. Toothpaste. The gray shirt, which is soft from washing, softer than anything I deserve.

Twenty minutes later, I'm on the 1 train heading downtown, the hum still there, a low drone behind my sternum like tinnitus that won't quit. I press my back against the tunnel wall and focus on the rhythm of the rails. The train is half-empty. A man in a paint-splattered apron reads a paperback. A woman argues softly into her phone in Korean. Nobody looks at me. I am very good at not being looked at.

The shop is on West 4th, squeezed between a wine bar and a place that sells artisanal candles. The window display changes weekly — this week it's a 1960s cocktail dress, ivory and moss-green, floating on an invisible mannequin against a velvet backdrop. A hand-lettered sign reads *Ephemera — Vintage & Curated*.

The bell above the door chimes when I enter. The shop smells the way it always does — cedar, old lace, the faint ghosts of perfumes that outlived their wearers. Racks of garments line the walls, arranged not by era but by color, which is how Sable's mind works. Right now the spectrum runs from ivory through rose to deepest burgundy. It looks like a bruise healing.

Sable is behind the counter, cataloguing — a loupe pressed to her eye, a pair of white cotton gloves on her hands, a silk slip spread on the black felt mat before her. She's sixty-three, Creole, with close-cropped silver hair and reading glasses pushed up on her forehead. She wears a simple black linen dress and her grandmother's gold hoops, and she has the kind of presence that makes rooms adjust to her rather than the other way around.
She looks up. The loupe drops. She takes one look at my face — the shadows under my eyes, the rawness of my throat, the way I'm holding myself like something that might shatter — and says:
"What did you do?"
It's not a question with multiple answers. It's the question she's been asking me, in various forms, for thirty-seven years.
"I opened my mouth at an auction."
"Orlando."

"At a fancy one. There was a coat. A 1940s utility coat — they had the provenance wrong. I corrected them." I follow her to the back of the shop, through the curtain of wooden beads she's never replaced because her mother hung them there in 1989. "Publicly."
"How publicly?"
"On the ballroom floor. In front of the buyer."
She stops walking. Turns. Her face is unreadable, which means she's furious. "And the buyer was—"
"Ashford. The Ashford Foundation."
Her eyes close, briefly. When she opens them, the fury has been filed into something more precise. "Come through to the archive. I need to sit down."
The shop's front is public. The back is not.

Beyond the beaded curtain, through a narrow hallway hung with framed photographs — Sable's grandmother, her mother, her aunties, each woman positioned in front of this same building, this same door — there is a door that looks like a closet. Sable keys in a six-digit code, and the lock disengages with a sound like a quiet confession.
The archive.

It's the size of a small apartment — climate-controlled, windowless, the air kept at a precise temperature and humidity that Sable monitors with a devotion that borders on prayer. The lighting is soft and even, LED strips that simulate the cool clarity of north-facing studio light. And along every wall, floor to ceiling, are the cases.
Archival glass. Each panel cold to the touch, each one a window into a life — *my* life, laid out garment by garment like evidence in a trial I didn't agree to.

The Venetian silk is closest to the door. 1743. A man's waistcoat, rose-pink and cream, embroidered with silver thread in a pattern of oak leaves and hunting dogs. I wore it to a carnival in the Campo San Samuele, the night I spent three hours arguing with a glass-blower about whether the soul has a shape. The silk still holds the faintest scent — amber and bergamot and something older, something like canal water and stone. I press my fingertips to the glass, feeling its coolness, and the hum shifts pitch.
"I saw him again, Sable."
Sable has lowered herself into the archive's single chair, the one with the velvet cushion that belonged to her mother. She looks up at me sharply. "Him?"

"Someone with his face. At the auction last night." I'm still touching the glass. I can't seem to stop. "He bought the coat. He — saw me. What I did. What I knew. He came over. We—" I stop. How do I describe it? The way his eyes held mine across that room. The way the hum roared and then went quiet, quiet like the stillness inside a bell after it's been struck. "He looked at me like he recognized something."
Sable goes very still. That archivist's stillness — the one that comes from decades of handling things that crumble if you breathe wrong. "Orlando. You know what proximity does."
"I know what everything does. That's rather the problem."
She doesn't laugh. I didn't expect her to.

I move along the wall. The Victorian mourning black — 1867. A dress I wore for three years straight, because I was mourning too, though not for the reason anyone assumed. The glass over it is immaculate; the bodice beneath, jet-beaded and severe, looks like it could be worn tomorrow. Then the WWII utility coat — not the auction coat, an earlier one, heavier, standard-issue, the one I wore when I walked across a bombed-out street in Clerkenwell and felt, for the first time in centuries, the specific fear of a mortal body. My body wasn't mortal. But the woman next to me was, and she wasn't walking anymore.

Then the linen tunic. 1994. Simple, cream-colored, soft as a second skin. I wore it to a hospital room where I sat holding a hand that was too thin, too light, and the beeping stopped in the middle of a Tuesday afternoon and I couldn't — I still can't—
I pull my hand back from the glass.
"Come sit down," Sable says.
"I don't want to sit down."
"Then stand. But stop pacing like you're going to wear a groove in my floor."
I stop pacing. The archive has that effect. You can't be careless in here. Every movement feels like it could damage something that took a century to preserve.

Sable watches me, her hands folded in her lap. The same hands that have catalogued every garment in this room, written condition reports, repaired tears with stitches smaller than a pinhead. The same hands that held mine the last time I came in here shaking, which was 2015, which was the last time I let myself think about the visions as anything other than a malfunction I'd outgrown.
"Design under your own name," she says.
My stomach drops. "Sable—"

"I'm serious. I've been serious for twenty years. You're the most talented designer I've ever encountered, and I've encountered *centuries* of fashion, Orlando. Because of you. What you've made is extraordinary. But you keep giving it away. Other people's labels. Other people's names. You hide behind contracts and NDAs and that maddening humility that isn't humility at all — it's cowardice dressed up in linen."
"Visibility is how I lose everything. Every time."

"You've lost things because of time. Because of what you are. Not because people saw your work." She leans forward, and her voice drops to the register she uses for the oldest, most fragile pieces in the archive. "Anonymity is how you've already lost it. You've been invisible for so long you've started to believe that's the same as surviving."

The argument is old. Decades old — since 1991, to be exact, when she found a sketch I'd discarded in a bin behind a fabric shop on Orchard Street and recognized the hand from the construction notes she'd found in her grandmother's files. She tracked me down in three days. She was twenty-six and fierce and I was — what I've always been — tired.
"You don't understand," I say, but even to my own ears it sounds like a line I've rehearsed too many times.

"I understand that this archive exists because my grandmother promised your secret would be kept. I understand that my mother took up that promise, and I've taken it up in turn. I understand what you are and what that costs." She stands. She's shorter than me by four inches, but right now she feels taller. "What I don't understand is why being alive for this long has made you so afraid of being *seen*."

"Because seeing leads to staying, and staying leads to watching, and watching leads to—" I gesture at the cases. At the Venetian silk. At the mourning dress. At the linen tunic. "To *that*. A room full of things I used to wear when I used to be someone, and I can't even look at them without—"
My voice cracks. I hate that it cracks.
Sable's expression softens. Not pity — she's never pitied me, I wouldn't let her — but something adjacent. The kind of tenderness that has to be hard-won.
"You could start again," she says. "That's the part you keep forgetting. You could start again, and this time, it could be *yours*."
"I can't."
"You won't."
"Same thing."
"It is not." She touches my arm, brief and firm. "Go home. Sleep. Drink something that isn't coffee. And think about what I've said."
"I always think about what you say."
"Think about it *differently*."

I turn to leave because I've turned to leave a thousand times and it's the only exit I know how to find. Through the archive. Past the cases. Past the Venetian silk under glass, still holding the ghost of my cologne from a carnival in 1743. Past the door with the six-digit code. Through the hallway of photographed women who kept my secret before Sable was born.
I'm almost at the shop's front entrance when the bell chimes.
A courier. Young, helmet under one arm, a clipboard in the other. He looks at Sable. "Ephemera? Delivery for the owner."

"I'm the owner." Sable takes the clipboard, signs, and receives a sealed envelope — heavy cream stock, the kind that costs more than paper should. She turns it over in her hands, and something in her expression shifts. She looks at me.
"It's for you."
"I don't—"
"Your name isn't on it. But it says 'the woman from the Ashford auction.'"
The room contracts. The hum, which had settled into something almost bearable, surges — a sound like a struck tuning fork, resonating in the hollow spaces between my ribs.
I take the envelope. The paper is thick between my fingers. I slide a nail under the seal — it cracks open with a sound like a small bone breaking — and unfold the letter inside.

*Ashford Foundation*
*R. Ashford, Director*
The letterhead is formal, typed, dated yesterday. A commission request. A bespoke piece — "designed by the woman from the auction" — for a private exhibition. A phone number. A deadline. Four weeks.
I stare at the emerald crest embossed at the top of the page.
Emerald. Set in gold. A specific crest — the one I saw in the flash, the one on the ring in the great hall with the firelight and the timber beams and the smell of woodsmoke and rain on stone.
The hum spikes. My hand doesn't shake. I won't let it.
"Orlando?" Sable's voice, careful and close. "What is it?"

I fold the letter. I slide it back into the envelope. The envelope goes into my coat pocket — the coat I'm wearing now, a thrift-store wool thing, nothing special, nothing that could ever end up under archival glass.

I should throw it away. I should hand it back to Sable and tell her to burn it, which she would do, because she has burned things for me before, because that is what her family does — they keep my secrets, and sometimes that means destroying the evidence.
But the emerald glows behind my eyelids. And the hum, for the first time in nine years, isn't just noise.
It's a direction.
"Nothing," I say. "It's nothing."

Sable watches me with those cataloguer's eyes, and I know she sees the lie, and I know she won't call it by name, because she's her mother's daughter and her grandmother's granddaughter, and the first rule of the archive is that you don't touch what isn't ready to be handled.
I push through the door into the June sunlight. The letter is a small, heavy weight against my hip.
I don't take it out again. Not yet.
